has_interactive_desktop.description:
This is a *session-level* value, not specific to an individual display.

It will be `false` in all non-interactive sessions, such as:

- SSH
- Remote console
- Headless server

TODO(acdvorak): Further clarify the difference between  {@link  has_interactive_desktop }  and {@is_attached_to_desktop}.

is_virtual_machine.description:
This is a *session-level* value, not specific to an individual display.

Best-effort signal that this display is *probably* running in a VM guest.

This is a *session-level* value, not specific to an individual display.

friendly_name.description: Manufacturer-provided model name from the DisplayID or EDID.
friendly_name.markdownDescription: Manufacturer-provided model name from the DisplayID or EDID.

short_lived_identifier.description:
Windows "monitor device name" from `MONITORINFOEX.szDevice`.

⚠️ NOT stable across device disconnects/reconnects.
short_lived_identifier.markdownDescription:
Windows "monitor device name" from `MONITORINFOEX.szDevice`.

⚠️ NOT stable across device disconnects/reconnects.

normalized_join_key.description:
Normalized join key, with trailing `_0` removed from  {@link  wmi_instance_name } .

E.g.:
- "DISPLAY\\SAM73A5\\5&21e6c3e1&0&UID5243153"
- "DISPLAY\\DELF023\\5&21e6c3e1&0&UID5243152"
normalized_join_key.markdownDescription:
Normalized join key, with trailing `_0` removed from
 {@link  wmi_instance_name } .

E.g.:
- "DISPLAY\\SAM73A5\\5&21e6c3e1&0&UID5243153"
- "DISPLAY\\DELF023\\5&21e6c3e1&0&UID5243152"
normalized_join_key.fullDescription:
Normalized join key, with trailing `_0` removed from
{@link wmi_instance_name}.

E.g.:

is_attached_to_desktop.description:
This value MIGHT be `false` under the following conditions:

- Unused connectors on the GPU:   - Many drivers expose one IDXGIOutput per physical connector     (HDMI/DP/DVI), even if nothing is plugged in.   - Those "ports" can enumerate, but they are not part of the desktop, so     AttachedToDesktop is false.

- A monitor is connected but disabled in Display Settings:   - Example: you have 2 monitors connected, but Windows is set to     "Show only on 1" (or you’ve "Disconnect this display" for the other).     That other output can still exist, but it is not attached, so false.

TODO(acdvorak): Further clarify the difference between  {@link  has_interactive_desktop  }  and {@is_attached_to_desktop}.
is_attached_to_desktop.markdownDescription:
This value MIGHT be `false` under the following conditions:

- Unused connectors on the GPU:
  - Many drivers expose one IDXGIOutput per physical connector
    (HDMI/DP/DVI), even if nothing is plugged in.
  - Those "ports" can enumerate, but they are not part of the desktop, so
    AttachedToDesktop is false.

- A monitor is connected but disabled in Display Settings:
  - Example: you have 2 monitors connected, but Windows is set to
    "Show only on 1" (or you’ve "Disconnect this display" for the other).
    That other output can still exist, but it is not attached, so false.

TODO(acdvorak): Further clarify the difference between
 {@link  has_interactive_desktop  }  and {@is_attached_to_desktop}.
is_attached_to_desktop.fullDescription:
This value MIGHT be `false` under the following conditions:

- Unused connectors on the GPU:
  - Many drivers expose one IDXGIOutput per physical connector
    (HDMI/DP/DVI), even if nothing is plugged in.
  - Those "ports" can enumerate, but they are not part of the desktop, so
    AttachedToDesktop is false.

- A monitor is connected but disabled in Display Settings:
  - Example: you have 2 monitors connected, but Windows is set to
    "Show only on 1" (or you’ve "Disconnect this display" for the other).
    That other output can still exist, but it is not attached, so false.

TODO(acdvorak): Further clarify the difference between
{@link has_interactive_desktop} and {@is_attached_to_desktop}.

dpi_scaling_percent.description:
The most common standard values are: `100 | 125 | 150 | 175 | 200`.

The user can technically set any arbitrary value via registry hacks.
dpi_scaling_percent.markdownDescription:
The most common standard values are: `100 | 125 | 150 | 175 | 200`.

The user can technically set any arbitrary value via registry hacks.

bounds.description:
The full size and position of the display, *including* the taskbar and any other areas that are not usable by maximized (non-fullscreen) applications.

Might not be available in some edge cases like virtual desktops.
bounds.markdownDescription:
The full size and position of the display, *including* the taskbar and any
other areas that are not usable by maximized (non-fullscreen) applications.

Might not be available in some edge cases like virtual desktops.
bounds.fullDescription:
The full size and position of the display, *including* the taskbar and any
other areas that are not usable by maximized (non-fullscreen) applications.

Might not be available in some edge cases like virtual desktops.

working_area.description:
Available working area on the screen.

This *excludes* taskbars and other docked windows.
working_area.markdownDescription:
Available working area on the screen.

This *excludes* taskbars and other docked windows.
working_area.fullDescription:
Available working area on the screen.

This *excludes* taskbars and other docked windows.

physical_connector_type.description: Physical connector type, if applicable (HDMI, DVI, DisplayPort, etc.).
physical_connector_type.markdownDescription: Physical connector type, if applicable (HDMI, DVI, DisplayPort, etc.).
physical_connector_type.fullDescription: Physical connector type, if applicable (HDMI, DVI, DisplayPort, etc.).

WinMonitorDeviceName.description:
On Windows, `System.Windows.Forms.Screen.DeviceName` is basically the Win "monitor device name" string (the `szDevice` field of `MONITORINFOEX`, filled by `GetMonitorInfo`).

In the common case, that comes out as the familiar GDI device path form:
- `\\.\DISPLAY1`
- `\\.\DISPLAY2`
- ... (1-based index, can go higher than 2)

WinForms historically also has a special-case fallback for "single monitor" and "no multimon" where it sets the device name to:
- `DISPLAY` (no `\\.\` prefix)

You can see both facts in the `WinForms` reference source:
- single-monitor path sets `"DISPLAY"`
- multi-monitor path uses `MONITORINFOEX.szDevice`

NOTE: Technically, This string may contain arbitrary non-printable characters according to Microsoft Learn.
WinMonitorDeviceName.markdownDescription:
On Windows, `System.Windows.Forms.Screen.DeviceName` is basically the Win
"monitor device name" string (the `szDevice` field of `MONITORINFOEX`,
filled by `GetMonitorInfo`).

In the common case, that comes out as the familiar GDI device path form:
- `\\.\DISPLAY1`
- `\\.\DISPLAY2`
- ... (1-based index, can go higher than 2)

WinForms historically also has a special-case fallback for "single monitor"
and "no multimon" where it sets the device name to:
- `DISPLAY` (no `\\.\` prefix)

You can see both facts in the `WinForms` reference source:
- single-monitor path sets `"DISPLAY"`
- multi-monitor path uses `MONITORINFOEX.szDevice`

NOTE: Technically, This string may contain arbitrary non-printable characters
according to Microsoft Learn.
WinMonitorDeviceName.fullDescription:
On Windows, `System.Windows.Forms.Screen.DeviceName` is basically the Win
"monitor device name" string (the `szDevice` field of `MONITORINFOEX`,
filled by `GetMonitorInfo`).

In the common case, that comes out as the familiar GDI device path form:
- `\\.\DISPLAY1`
- `\\.\DISPLAY2`
- ... (1-based index, can go higher than 2)

WinForms historically also has a special-case fallback for "single monitor"
and "no multimon" where it sets the device name to:
- `DISPLAY` (no `\\.\` prefix)

You can see both facts in the `WinForms` reference source:
- single-monitor path sets `"DISPLAY"`
- multi-monitor path uses `MONITORINFOEX.szDevice`

NOTE: Technically, This string may contain arbitrary non-printable characters
according to Microsoft Learn.

@see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.windows.forms.screen.devicename?view=windowsdesktop-10.0
@see https://github.com/dotnet/dotnet/blob/3bc68b106/src/winforms/src/System.Windows.Forms/System/Windows/Forms/Screen.cs#L63
@see https://github.com/dotnet/dotnet/blob/3bc68b106/src/winforms/src/System.Windows.Forms/System/Windows/Forms/Screen.cs#L78

WinScreenRectangle.description: Rectangle payload used by Bounds and WorkingArea.
WinScreenRectangle.markdownDescription: Rectangle payload used by Bounds and WorkingArea.
WinScreenRectangle.fullDescription: Rectangle payload used by Bounds and WorkingArea.

WinDisplayConnectorType.description:
Values with "embedded" in their names indicate that the graphics adapter's video output device connects internally to the display device.

In those cases, the `DISPLAYCONFIG_OUTPUT_TECHNOLOGY_INTERNAL` value is redundant. The caller should ignore `DISPLAYCONFIG_OUTPUT_TECHNOLOGY_INTERNAL` and just process the embedded values, `DISPLAYCONFIG_OUTPUT_TECHNOLOGY_DISPLAYPORT_EMBEDDED` and `DISPLAYCONFIG_OUTPUT_TECHNOLOGY_UDI_EMBEDDED`.

An embedded display port or UDI is also known as an integrated display port or UDI.
WinDisplayConnectorType.markdownDescription:
Values with "embedded" in their names indicate that the graphics adapter's
video output device connects internally to the display device.

In those cases, the `DISPLAYCONFIG_OUTPUT_TECHNOLOGY_INTERNAL` value is
redundant. The caller should ignore
`DISPLAYCONFIG_OUTPUT_TECHNOLOGY_INTERNAL` and just process the embedded
values, `DISPLAYCONFIG_OUTPUT_TECHNOLOGY_DISPLAYPORT_EMBEDDED` and
`DISPLAYCONFIG_OUTPUT_TECHNOLOGY_UDI_EMBEDDED`.

An embedded display port or UDI is also known as an integrated display port
or UDI.
WinDisplayConnectorType.fullDescription:
Values with "embedded" in their names indicate that the graphics adapter's
video output device connects internally to the display device.

In those cases, the `DISPLAYCONFIG_OUTPUT_TECHNOLOGY_INTERNAL` value is
redundant. The caller should ignore
`DISPLAYCONFIG_OUTPUT_TECHNOLOGY_INTERNAL` and just process the embedded
values, `DISPLAYCONFIG_OUTPUT_TECHNOLOGY_DISPLAYPORT_EMBEDDED` and
`DISPLAYCONFIG_OUTPUT_TECHNOLOGY_UDI_EMBEDDED`.

An embedded display port or UDI is also known as an integrated display port
or UDI.

@see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/wingdi/ne-wingdi-displayconfig_video_output_technology

WmiVideoOutputTechnology.description:
Type of physical connector a video output device (on the display adapter) uses to connect to an external display device.

Known values for  {@link  WmiMonitorConnectionParams.VideoOutputTechnology  } .
WmiVideoOutputTechnology.markdownDescription:
Type of physical connector a video output device (on the display adapter)
uses to connect to an external display device.

Known values for  {@link  WmiMonitorConnectionParams.VideoOutputTechnology  } .
WmiVideoOutputTechnology.fullDescription:
Type of physical connector a video output device (on the display adapter)
uses to connect to an external display device.

Known values for {@link WmiMonitorConnectionParams.VideoOutputTechnology}.

@see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-hardware/drivers/ddi/d3dkmdt/ne-d3dkmdt-_d3dkmdt_video_output_technology
